X: One yes means one sentence. Beauty alone doesn't carry weight; the detail doing the work stays, and the rest goes to your notes.
:::

## Cutting in revision: almost always more than you think

A useful exercise on a stuck chapter. Cut every adjective. Read the chapter back.

Almost all of the cuts will read better than the original. The few that don't, restore.

The same for adverbs. The same for sentences whose only function is description. A novel that has been through this pass once is a novel whose pace has just gone up by twenty percent.

Most beginners fight this exercise because the cuts feel like loss. They aren't. They are *concentration*: the same word the cut exercise used in Module 3. The two sentences of description that survive the cut land far harder than the five would have.

:::quote
The painful part of writing is not the writing. It is the cutting.
:::

A writer who has not learned to cut is a writer producing a draft no agent will finish.

:::video oPwVsfgEqrU | How and Why to Skip Time in Your Novel | Ellen Brock

## What this gives you

The 1-sentence room as the default.

The load-bearing-detail test for every passage of description in your draft.

The skill of embedding description in action so it carries character at the same time.

The discipline to cut more than you think in revision: the single most reliable upgrade available to a first draft.

This is the last lesson of Module 4. You now have, for your draft: the show/tell choice (L1), the multi-sensory technique (L2), the elevated setting when the story can hold it (L3), and the discipline to keep all of it lean (L4).

The next module turns from the *world* of the novel to the *process* of writing one. The blank page. The first draft. Revision. When to stop.

## Try it

:::exercise
**Exercise 4.4.1: Highlight and decide.** Open a chapter you've written. Highlight every passage of description longer than two sentences.

For each, apply the load-bearing-detail test:

- Does this description tell the reader about the *character*?
- Does this description *set up* a later moment?
- Does this description carry *atmosphere* the scene needs?

One yes: cut to a single sentence. Zero yeses: cut entirely. Three yeses: leave it.

Read the chapter back. If it gained pace and kept meaning, that's the lesson. If it lost meaning, restore *only* the descriptions that were doing the lost work, and only those.
:::

:::takeaway
- The cure for slow description is not less description; it is load-bearing description.
- The default for any room is one sentence; only load-bearing rooms earn elevation.
- The strongest description is embedded in action, where it carries character at the same time.
- In revision, cut more than you think: the details that survive land harder for the company they lost.
:::

## Further reading

All public domain. Free at Project Gutenberg.

- *Hard Times* (Dickens, 1854). For the contrast: lavish setting description in the *Coketown* paragraphs, set against tight, action-driven scenes elsewhere. The same author moving between elevated and tight description in the same novel. ([gutenberg.org/ebooks/786](https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/786))
- *Pride and Prejudice* (Austen, 1813). Short on standalone description; embedded everywhere. A textbook in the embedded-description technique. ([gutenberg.org/ebooks/1342](https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1342))
- *A Christmas Carol* (Dickens, 1843). Elevated description used at exactly the right places: Scrooge's office, his bedchamber, the spirit's tableau. Each elevation marks a structural beat. The same author choosing where to lavish and where to be sparse, in a story short enough to read in an evening. ([gutenberg.org/ebooks/46](https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/46))

lessons.title: The blank page (overcoming it)
lessons.content:
# The blank page (overcoming it)

Sitting and not writing is not the same as choosing not to write.

The fear is real. The perfectionism is real. The voice that says *anything you put down will be worse than nothing* is real.

This lesson is about working through it. Mechanically. Not with pep talks.

## The blank page is real, but "writer's block" is the wrong name

The phrase *writer's block* hides the diagnosis.

Most blank pages are one of four specific things. Naming the cause is half the cure.

**Fear of being bad.** You are protecting yourself from producing something embarrassing. The worst sentence on the page is worse than the *idea* of the best sentence in your head, so you keep the page blank.

**Perfectionism.** You are waiting for the right sentence before starting. The right sentence does not arrive on a blank page. It arrives on the third bad sentence.

**Unclear premise.** You cannot draft a story whose engine you haven't found yet. Callback to [Module 1, Lesson 3](/learn/lesson/foundation-finding-idea): when the blank page is *information* about the premise, the cure is at premise level, not at typing level.

**No plan.** You don't know what *this scene* is supposed to do. You're trying to draft without knowing the function.

Each cause has a different fix. The next sections are the three tactics that work for the first two, and the test for when the cause is the third or fourth.

:::video sRW87vsC90I | Why You Keep Starting New Novels Instead of Finishing One | Ellen Brock

## Three tactics that work

**Start in the middle of a scene.**

Skip the throat-clearing. The first paragraph of any chapter usually gets cut anyway; write the second paragraph first, where the scene is already moving.

> He set the cup down and said *I told you I would* before she finished asking the question.

You're already in. The paragraph before this one (wherever they were, however he came in) can be filled in later or never. The important sentence is the one where something is happening.

**Write the easy scene first.**

The scene you've been imagining for months, the one you can already see in your head, is not necessarily chapter one. That's fine.

Drafting in order is a convention, not a rule. The momentum from a scene you wrote well will carry you into the harder ones. Most beginner writers waste two months trying to write a difficult opening because the opening "should" come first; the opening can be written in the third week of drafting, after the easy scenes have built the writer's confidence in the project.

**Write a terrible first paragraph on purpose.**

Aim for bad. Compete with yourself for the worst opening sentence you can produce. *The morning was a morning, and Sarah was Sarah, and she did the thing that Saturdays make a person do.* Worse than that, if you can.

:::quote
The fear of being bad cannot survive the requirement to be bad.
:::

Once the page is no longer blank, the second paragraph is a different problem.

:::check
Q: You have spent two months stuck on your opening chapter because the opening "should" come first. What does this lesson tell you to do?
- Keep at it; a novel drafted out of order loses its momentum
-* Draft the scene you can already see in your head and write the opening later
- Cut the opening chapter; first chapters usually get deleted anyway
X: Drafting in order is a convention, not a rule. Momentum from a scene you write well carries you into the harder ones, and the opening can be written in the third week of drafting.
:::

## Constraints help

A blank page in front of unlimited time is harder than a blank page with a 25-minute timer.

**The timer.** Twenty-five minutes is short enough that the inner critic doesn't fully wake up. Longer sessions are useful; the critic learns to talk over them.

**The minimum word count.** Most writers do better with a small daily target they cannot fail (250 words) than a large one they sometimes hit (2,000). The target is for the *habit*; the habit is for the book. The next lesson is about that habit.

**The skipped chapter.** If the chapter you're supposed to write next won't come, write the chapter after it. Mark the missing chapter with a placeholder. Come back when the surrounding pages have told you what the missing one needs to do.

## When the blank page is telling you something

Sometimes you are not blocked. Sometimes the premise isn't ready, or the scene doesn't have a function yet.

Sign: when you sit down to write the *same* scene three days in a row and produce nothing each time, the issue is rarely fear. The issue is usually that the scene doesn't know what it's for.

Cure: leave the page. Go back to outline level. Ask of the scene: *what does the protagonist want here, what's in the way, what changes by the end?* (Callback to [Module 1, Lesson 4](/learn/lesson/foundation-three-ingredients): the three ingredients aren't only for the whole novel; every scene is a small story with the same architecture.) When you can answer those three questions, the scene will write itself in twenty minutes.

The blank page is not always a failure of will. Sometimes it's the writer's instinct telling you the work isn't ready. Listen for that.

:::check
Q: You sit down to write the *same* scene three days in a row and produce nothing each time. What is the most likely cause?
- Fear of being bad; write a terrible first paragraph on purpose
- A broken habit; lower your daily word count until it is unfailable
-* The scene has no clear function yet; leave the page and fix it at outline level
X: Repeated failure on the same specific scene is rarely fear. It usually means the scene does not know what it is for, and the cure lives at outline level, not typing level.
:::

## What this gives you

The blank page is a real problem. It is not unsolvable. The next lesson is about the longer version of the same question: once you have started, how do you keep going for six months.

## Try it

:::exercise
**Exercise 5.1.1: The 25-minute, 500-word session.** Set a timer for twenty-five minutes. Open a fresh document. Write 500 words on any scene from your story.

Rules:

- Do not stop typing.
- Do not edit while writing.
- Allowed: rambling, repetition, confusion, *[notes to self in brackets]*, restarting the same sentence three times.

The session does not produce *publishable* prose. It produces *prose*. That's what you need.

If you produce 500 words, the page is no longer blank. If you produce 200 words, the page is no longer blank. Tomorrow's session is easier. The lesson is mechanical.
:::

:::takeaway
- Most blank pages have one of four causes: fear of being bad, perfectionism, an unclear premise, or no plan for the scene. Name the cause first.
- Fear and perfectionism respond to tactics: start in the middle of a scene, write the easy scene first, or write a terrible paragraph on purpose.
- Constraints beat willpower: a 25-minute timer and a small unfailable word count outwork unlimited time.
- If the same scene fails three days running, leave the page and fix the scene's function at outline level.
:::

## Further reading

- *An Autobiography* (Anthony Trollope, 1883). Brief, direct, public-domain. Trollope wrote two hundred and fifty words every quarter-hour before walking to his job at the Post Office, and produced forty-seven novels by the end of his life. The chapter on his daily routine is the disenchantment of *blocked writer* as a category. Once you've read it, the word *blocked* never quite means the same thing again. ([gutenberg.org/ebooks/5978](https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/5978))

lessons.title: Writing the first draft (permission to be bad)
lessons.content:
# Writing the first draft (permission to be bad)

The first draft is for you. Nobody else will ever see it.

Publishers see revised drafts. Agents see polished pages. Beta readers see something further along. The first draft has one reader, and that reader is the writer doing the next pass.

This is liberating.

:::quote
The first draft does not have to be good. It has to exist.
:::

Existence is the harder problem.

## The two voices

Every writer has two voices in their head when working.

**Writer-mode** generates. It is willing to put down the wrong word. It is willing to write a scene that won't make the cut. It is willing to spend an afternoon on a chapter the writer will probably delete.

**Editor-mode** judges. It catches the wrong word. It refuses to keep going until the previous sentence is right. It is correct most of the time, which is the problem.

The two cannot run at once.

Editor-mode running during writer-mode is the most common reason a first draft never finishes. Every sentence is judged before the next one arrives. The judging slows the writing past the rate at which a book gets written. The writer feels they are working hard (every sentence is being polished) and the page count creeps up by ten words a day.

The fix is mechanical:

- Do not edit the previous paragraph.
- Do not delete sentences.
- Do not re-read what you wrote yesterday before adding to it. (Or only briefly. A glance, not a session.)
- If editor-mode keeps interrupting, set a timer and forbid editing until the timer ends. Twenty-five minutes is enough.

Editor-mode comes back during revision, when it is welcome. The next three lessons are when.

:::check
Q: Halfway through a drafting session you notice yesterday's chapter opens with a weak paragraph. What does this lesson say to do?
-* Leave it alone and keep drafting; editing comes back in revision
- Fix it now, while you can still see the problem clearly
- Delete the chapter and restart it from the strongest paragraph
X: Editor-mode running during writer-mode is the most common reason a first draft never finishes. The weak paragraph will still be there in revision, when fixing it is welcome.
:::

## The bad first draft is the standard

Almost every first draft is bad.

Famous writers say this in interviews and almost no beginners believe it. Believe it.

The drafts you read on shelves have been through three to ten passes by the writer, then more by editors. The writer who put the first draft into print writes a different kind of book than the one you are trying to make.

This does not mean *try to be bad*. It means *don't expect to be good yet*. The good comes in revision. The first draft is the raw material the revision works on.

A first draft you'd be embarrassed to show anyone is a first draft that has done its job. A first draft you're proud of after one pass is usually a first draft you've been polishing instead of finishing, and the embarrassment was, paradoxically, the cost-free path through.

## The daily habit: small, sustainable, unfailable

The single most reliable upgrade to a writing life is a daily wordcount you cannot fail.

Pick a number low enough that you'd be embarrassed to miss it.

Two hundred and fifty words is fine. Five hundred is fine. A thousand is on the high side; many writers can't sustain it, and an unsustained habit is worse than a sustained small one.

Anthony Trollope wrote two hundred and fifty words every quarter-hour, every morning, before walking to his job at the Post Office. He kept a watch on his desk. He produced forty-seven novels.

The number was small. The consistency wasn't.

The habit is not about output. It is about *finishing*. Most novels die at month three because the writer skipped a week, then two weeks, then a month, and never came back. The habit makes the book inevitable. It is not glamorous. Writers' descriptions of their morning routines are mostly identical and mostly boring; that's the point.

:::video E-gTGKdu4uw | How to Win the Marathon of the Middle | Jerry B. Jenkins

If you find yourself negotiating with the count (*I'll do 500 today and 0 tomorrow because I'm tired*) lower the count. The number you do every day is the right number. The number you sometimes hit and sometimes don't is the wrong number, no matter how big.

:::check
Q: Which daily word count is the right one?
- The highest number you can hit on a good day
- A round 1,000, because that is what professionals sustain
-* The number you hit every single day without negotiating
X: The habit exists to finish the book, and a target you sometimes miss trains you to skip. The number you do every day is the right number, no matter how small.
:::

## Outline vs discovery write

Some writers outline before drafting. Some discover the story by writing it. Both work.

**Outlining** suits writers who want a route map. The work in [Module 2](/learn/module/architecture) (three acts, the hero's journey, beat sheets) is here for *use* in this draft, not for enforcement. An outline is a hypothesis about your story, not a contract.

**Discovery writing** suits writers who find their plot by drafting it. The trade-off: discovery drafts are often messier, longer, and require deeper structural revision. Plan accordingly.

**The hybrid** most professional writers use: a loose outline of major beats (inciting incident, lock-in, midpoint, dark moment, climax, callback to [Module 2, Lesson 1](/learn/lesson/architecture-three-acts)) and discovery writing the scenes between them.

The choice is one of temperament. There is no right answer. The wrong answer is to insist on a method that fights how your mind actually works. A discovery writer trying to outline a fifty-page beat sheet will stall at week two; an outliner trying to discovery-write 80,000 words will produce a directionless mess. Use the method that lets you finish.

:::video 4hp_7e3E7CY | How to Fix a Saggy Middle | Ellen Brock

## First-draft killers

Four common reasons a first draft dies in progress:

**Editing as you go.** Slows the writing past the rate at which the book gets finished. Drafts die here.

**Showing drafts too early.** A first draft shown to a reader before completion almost always gets feedback the writer can't act on, and the feedback derails the draft. Wait. Even the people closest to you. Especially them.

**Comparing to published work.** The published novel is on its tenth pass. Your first draft is on its first. The comparison is dishonest; the writer punishing themselves with it stops writing. Keep the published novel on the shelf, not on the desk.

**Skipping the daily wordcount.** Once is fine. Twice in a week is the start of the slide. Three times in a week and the book is at risk.

## What this gives you

Permission, when you need it, to write the worst chapter of your life and continue.

The next lesson is the answer to *now what?*: what you do with the bad first draft you've finished.

## Try it

:::exercise
**Exercise 5.2.1: Seven days of small.** Set a daily word count you'd be embarrassed to fail. Two hundred and fifty is fine. Three hundred is better. *Do not pick five hundred unless you know you'll hit it for seven days.*

Write that count every day for seven days. The goal is not quality. The goal is the habit.

At the end of the week, you will have between 1,750 and 2,100 words and a much clearer sense of what your daily writing looks like when it isn't optional.

The writer who produces at this rate for a year produces a novel. The writer who skips three days a week never finishes one.
:::

:::takeaway
- Writer-mode generates and editor-mode judges. They cannot run at once, and drafts die when the editor interrupts the writer.
- Bad first drafts are the standard, not failure. The good comes in revision.
- Pick a daily word count you cannot fail. Small and sustained beats large and skipped.
- Outline, discovery write, or hybrid: the right method is whichever one lets you finish.
- Show the draft to no one until it is complete.
:::

## Further reading

- *An Autobiography* (Anthony Trollope, 1883). The single most useful pre-1900 description of professional writing as a practice, and the source of the postal-clerk-with-a-watch detail. Read the chapter on his daily routine alone. ([gutenberg.org/ebooks/5978](https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/5978))

lessons.title: Revision in 3 passes
lessons.content:
# Revision in 3 passes

You cannot fix a draft in one pass.

Structural problems and line-level problems live at different scales. Trying to see both at once is why writers spend three months polishing prose in chapters they will eventually cut.

The cure is sequence. Three passes, in this order. The sequence is the lesson; the rest of this module is what you do during each pass.

:::checklist
- Pass 1, structural: does the story work?
- Pass 2, scene: does every scene earn its place?
- Pass 3, line: does every sentence earn its words?
:::

## Why one pass doesn't work

You cannot simultaneously hold *does this chapter belong in the book* and *is this comma in the right place*.

They are different mental modes.

Structural mode is loose, abstract, willing to discard scenes. It looks at the manuscript as a *shape*. It reads fast.

Line mode is tight, specific, in love with the sentence. It looks at the manuscript as *prose*. It reads slow.

Run them at the same time and one wins. Usually line mode wins, because line mode produces visible progress (this paragraph is better!) while structural mode produces invisible progress (this chapter is gone).

The cost of running them simultaneously is wasted polish.

A perfectly worded paragraph in a chapter that will be cut is wasted work. Six months of wasted work is how a draft dies in revision.

:::quote
Don't fix sentences in chapters you'll cut.
:::

That is the spine of the lesson. Every other thing in this module is downstream of it.

:::video WEFC5Oz_Dco | How to Start Editing Your Novel | Ellen Brock

## Pass 1: structural

One question: *does the story work?*

Read the manuscript fast. The pace of the read matters: read it the way a stranger would, in three or four sittings, not over six weeks of careful study. You want the *shape* of the book, not the texture.

Take notes only on what isn't working at the chapter scale:

- Scenes that don't belong.
- Characters whose function is unclear.
- Structural beats that are missing or weak.
- Pacing problems at the act scale (sagging middle, rushed ending).

**Tool:** a single sheet of paper. One sentence per scene, summarising what that scene *does* for the story. If you cannot write the sentence, the scene isn't doing work.

The output of Pass 1 is a *list of structural changes*. Add a chapter. Cut a chapter. Move a chapter from after the midpoint to before it. Replace the antagonist's first appearance with a stronger one.

Don't fix anything yet. Write the list.

Then make the changes. Add the missing chapters. Cut the dead ones. Move what needs moving. Rewrite where needed *at the structural level only*, meaning rough scenes, not polished prose. The sentences will be terrible. That is correct. They are placeholders for Pass 2 to inhabit.

At the end of Pass 1 you have a draft whose architecture is sound. The prose may be awful in the chapters you rewrote. That's expected.

## Pass 2: scene-level

The question for each scene: *does this scene earn its place?*

A scene earns its place if it has the three ingredients from [Module 1](/learn/lesson/foundation-three-ingredients). Someone wants something. Something is in the way. Something has changed by the end.

Read the manuscript again, scene by scene. For each scene that does not have the three ingredients clearly, mark it. Either rewrite the scene to have them, or cut it.

Other Pass 2 checks:

- Does the scene start late and end early? Most beginner scenes start a paragraph too early and end a paragraph too late.
- Does at least one of the major structural beats from [Module 2](/learn/lesson/architecture-three-acts) (inciting incident, lock-in, midpoint, dark moment, climax) live in this scene? Not every scene; but the right scenes need to.
- Are the right characters present? Could two characters be merged? (Callback to [Module 3, Lesson 4](/learn/lesson/characters-supporting-cast).)
- Is the conflict in the scene specific, not generic?

Pass 2 produces scenes that work as scenes, in a manuscript whose structure is already sound.

The prose is *still* not polished. Pass 3 is where polish happens. Resist the urge to polish a sentence you encounter on Pass 2, even when the fix is obvious. A polished sentence in a scene Pass 3 will further revise is not free. It is a draw on the writer's attention budget that should be going to scene-level questions.

:::check
Q: On Pass 2 you find a clumsy sentence in a scene that clearly has all three ingredients. What do you do?
- Fix it now; the scene is staying, so the polish will not be wasted
-* Leave it; sentences belong to Pass 3, and polishing now spends attention Pass 2 needs
- Cut it; most clumsy sentences are stronger when deleted
X: Even a safe-looking polish is a draw on the attention budget that Pass 2 needs for scene-level questions. The sentence gets its turn on Pass 3.
:::

## Pass 3: line-level

Now, finally, the prose.

Read aloud. Read on paper. Read on a different device than the one you wrote on. The change of medium is the trick that lets you see what you've been re-reading without seeing.

Catch what the next lesson's checklist will catch: passive voice, repeated words, dialogue tag overuse, sentence rhythm, awkward grammar, any line the ear flinches at.

Cut more than you think (callback to [Module 4, Lesson 4](/learn/lesson/world-description-pace): *the painful part of writing is the cutting*). Most adverbs are stronger when cut. Most adjective-pairs should be one adjective. Most descriptions are one sentence too long.

Pass 3 is the polish. It is the smallest of the three passes by what it changes per page; it is the largest by what it requires per minute. Read slowly. Let the ear catch what the eye doesn't.

:::video 8PahOZhRSIw | How to Revise a Novel Step by Step | Abbie Emmons

## Time between passes

Let the draft cool.

Two weeks is the floor. Six is better.

You cannot revise a draft you can still recite. The mind reads what it expected to write, not what is actually on the page. Time off restores the ability to read what's there.

In the cooling period, write something else. Notes for the next book. A short story. Anything that re-engages writer-mode and lets editor-mode rest. The same principle applies between Pass 2 and Pass 3: a week off, even just five days, sharpens the line-level pass.

:::check
Q: Why does a draft need at least two weeks of cooling before the structural pass?
- Because the ideas need time to develop on their own
-* Because you cannot read what is on the page while you can still recite what you meant to write
- Because structural problems only become visible after beta readers respond
X: The mind reads what it expected to write, not what is actually there. Time off restores the ability to see the actual page.
:::

## Tools for re-seeing

A few mechanical tricks that catch what re-reading on the same screen doesn't:

:::checklist
- Print it: paper catches different problems than the screen
- Read on a different device: anything that breaks the visual pattern of the original document
- Read aloud: if your tongue trips on a sentence, the reader's brain trips on it too
- Read in a different room: sounds trivial, works
:::

These are not gimmicks. They exploit the same thing: a manuscript you have stopped seeing because you've been seeing it the same way for months.

## What this gives you

The next lesson is the *what to check, specifically* version of all three passes: a working checklist with a one-sentence reason for every item.

## Try it

:::exercise
**Exercise 5.3.1: Pass 1 only, on one chapter.** Take a chapter from your draft. On a single sheet of paper, write a one-sentence summary of what *each scene* in that chapter does for the story.

*What does this scene do? In one sentence.*

If you can write the sentence in plain English, the scene is doing work.

If you cannot, the scene is not yet earning its place. Mark it. *Don't fix it yet*. Pass 2 is where the fixes live. Pass 1 produces a list, not a rewrite.

Doing this for one chapter is a fifteen-minute exercise. Doing it for a whole manuscript is the structural pass.
:::

:::takeaway
- Revise in three passes, structural then scene then line, and never fix sentences in chapters you might cut.
- Pass 1 reads fast for shape and produces a list of structural changes, not fixes.
- Pass 2 makes every scene earn its place with want, obstacle, and change; Pass 3 polishes the prose, slowly and aloud.
- Let the draft cool for at least two weeks between passes; you cannot revise what you can still recite.
- Re-see the manuscript mechanically: paper, a different device, a different room, reading aloud.
:::

lessons.title: Self-editing checklist
lessons.content:
# Self-editing checklist

A checklist that doesn't tell you *why* each item matters is a checklist you can't adapt.

A writer who knows that *use vivid verbs* is on the list but doesn't know *why* will follow the rule on every page, including the pages where a flat verb is doing better work. A writer who knows the *why* applies the rule when it serves the prose and breaks it when it doesn't.

The rest of this lesson is a working checklist. About twenty items, organised by Pass (callback to [Lesson 3](/learn/lesson/process-revision-passes)). Every item is a yes/no question. Every item gets one sentence on why it matters.

:::video 6xS8LykRUj0 | How to Edit Your Novel: Advice from an Editor | Ellen Brock

## A checklist organised by pass

Three sections (Structural, Scene, Line) matching the three passes from L3. The order matters; structural items get checked first, line items last. Polishing prose in a chapter you'll cut is the failure mode the previous lesson warned you about.

The checklist is not exhaustive. It is *load-bearing*. Every item earns its place by catching a specific problem the earlier modules taught you to recognise. Most items are diagnostic in disguise: a question that, if you answer no, points to a specific fix.

:::check
Q: Why does every item on this checklist come with a *why*?
- So the list is easier to memorise
-* So you can adapt each rule to your work, and break it where it serves the prose
- So you can justify your edits to beta readers
X: A writer who knows only the rule follows it on every page, including pages where breaking it does better work. Knowing the why is what makes the rule adaptable.
:::

## Pass 1: structural

:::checklist
- Is the protagonist's want clear by chapter 2 or page 30? Readers form their investment from the want; without it they don't yet care what happens.
- Is there a clear inciting incident in Act 1? The disturbance to the normal is what makes Act 2 necessary; without it, the story keeps introducing.
- Is there a lock-in by the end of Act 1? Without a choice the protagonist cannot un-make, Act 2 has nothing to start from.
- Is there a midpoint reversal at roughly the middle? A sagging middle is almost always a missing midpoint.
- Is there a dark moment late in Act 2? The climax has nothing to climb out of without a floor in Act 2.
- Does the protagonist change by the end? A story whose situation changed but whose character didn't is a complaint, not a story.
- Does every act break feel earned? The reader should feel the story arrived here, not the writer needed an act break here.
:::

## Pass 2: scene-level

:::checklist
- Does this scene have the three ingredients? Without want, obstacle, and change it doesn't function as a unit of narrative; the reader senses filler.
- Does the scene start late and end early? Cutting the first and last paragraph of a draft scene usually improves it.
- Are the right characters present? A scene with two characters who could be one is a scene that hasn't been edited yet.
- Is the conflict in the scene specific, not generic? "Things go badly" is not a conflict; a specific want meeting a specific obstacle is.
- Does the scene either advance the plot or change the protagonist? If neither, the scene is filler, even if the prose is good.
- Is the setting doing work? The *where* should pull weight, or the location is decoration the reader will skim over.
:::

:::video q5S2pg3zgNE | Live Edit: Fixing a Boring Scene | Ellen Brock

## Pass 3: line-level

:::checklist
- Are dialogue tags mostly *said*? The eye trips on *exclaimed* and *muttered*; it does not trip on *said*. Variation reads as nervous; *said* reads as transparent.
- Are there fewer than three adverbs per page? An adverb often signals a verb that wasn't strong enough; the fix is usually a stronger verb.
- Are there fewer than two adjectives in a row? *The cold, dark, empty room* should be one of those words; pick the one that does the most work.
- Is the same word repeated within two paragraphs accidentally? Deliberate repetition works for emphasis; accidental repetition reads as carelessness.
- Are sentences varied in length? All-similar-length sentences read as monotonous; the ear hears the rhythm even when the reader doesn't notice.
- Is description load-bearing? Description that doesn't reveal character, set up something later, or carry needed atmosphere is cut.
- Does each shown scene engage at least two senses? A scene with only sight engaged feels flat to the reader even when they can't say why.
- Have you read this aloud? The ear catches what the eye doesn't; the most reliable single line-level diagnostic.
:::

## The "out loud" test

Most writers don't read aloud because it feels strange.

Read aloud anyway.

The strangeness is the lesson. Sentences your eye accepts your tongue refuses. A paragraph that scans well silently can have a clause buried in it that catches the breath halfway through. Out loud, you find it.

You don't need to read the whole manuscript aloud (that takes weeks). Read the chapters that feel slightly off. Read the scenes you're unsure about. Read the dialogue.

A reading-aloud session that lasts thirty minutes catches more line-level problems than three hours of silent re-reading.

## When to stop checking

The checklist is for one revision pass through the draft. Not for every paragraph forever.

After one full line-level pass with the checklist, stop.

The next lesson (*when to stop revising entirely*) is the deeper version of this question. The signal that you've over-applied the checklist is that you're moving the same fixes back and forth between passes: putting an adverb in, taking it out, putting it back. That's the curve telling you to stop.

## What this gives you

A working checklist organised by pass.

A reason for each item, so you can adapt the rule to your work, or break it where it serves the prose.

The discipline to apply the checklist *once* per pass, not forever.

The next lesson closes the module: how to know when the work is done.

## Try it

:::exercise
**One chapter, line-level pass.** Take a chapter you've already revised. Run it through the line-level checklist above, ticking each item as you go.

List the fixes you find here: places where dialogue tags are wrong, places where description doesn't earn its keep, places where adverbs could be cut.

The list is information about your *blind spots*. Most writers consistently miss the same two or three categories. Knowing which two or three is what lets you self-edit faster on the next book.
:::

:::takeaway
- A checklist without a why for each item is a checklist you cannot adapt; the why is what lets you break the rule where it serves the prose.
- Check in pass order: structural first, scene next, line last, so you never polish prose in a chapter you will cut.
- Every item is a yes/no question, and a no points to a specific fix.
- Reading aloud is the single most reliable line-level diagnostic; thirty minutes out loud beats three hours of silent re-reading.
- Apply the checklist once per pass, not forever; shuffling the same fixes back and forth is the signal to stop.
:::

lessons.title: When to stop revising
lessons.content:
# When to stop revising

Books are not finished. They are released.

A writer who cannot tell when to stop revising spends a year polishing one book while their next book waits unwritten. The cure is concrete signs (three on each side) plus the *next-book remedy*, which is the closer of this module and of the course.

This lesson has teeth. The signs are observable, not feelings.

## The diminishing returns curve

The first revision pass on a draft is transformative.

The second is large.

The third is medium.

The fourth is small.

By the eighth or tenth, you are moving commas back and forth without adding meaning.

This isn't laziness; it's the curve. The work is real and useful for the first three to five passes. After that, a writer can spend forever and not produce a meaningfully better book.

The skill is to *feel* the curve. Past a certain point, each new pass produces less gain than the same time would produce on a different book. The rest of this lesson is the diagnostic for where you are on the curve.

For a working writer's honest picture of what a full revision cycle feels like from the inside, including the moment it stops paying, watch this alongside the lesson.

:::video 65otc2uE7-Q | Revising a Novel: Process, Tips, and Tricks | ShaelinWrites

## Three signs you're done

**You're moving commas back and forth.**

You changed a comma in chapter five last week. This week you changed it back. Next week you'll change it again. The book is past the point where your edits add meaning. The edits are the *appearance* of progress; the manuscript is no longer changing in any way the reader will feel.

**You're rewriting passages you previously approved.**

A sentence you signed off on in pass three has been rewritten in pass seven. In pass eight, you'll rewrite it back to something close to pass three. The novel is not getting better in those passages; you are seeing the same passage with different eyes on different days. This is normal and unending. The cure is to stop.

**Beta readers stop having structural notes.**

Earlier readers said *this chapter is too slow* and *the antagonist isn't believable*. Recent readers are saying *I really liked the part where she chose*. When the structural feedback dries up, the structural work is done. What remains is taste, and taste is infinite. You'll never finish satisfying everyone's taste; the question is whether the structure works.

## Three signs you're not done

**You can't articulate what the book is about.**

If you cannot say what the novel is *for* (its question, its argument, its central tension) in two sentences, the book hasn't found itself yet. Revision is needed, and probably at the structural level (Pass 1 from [the previous lesson](/learn/lesson/process-revision-passes)).

**The ending feels rushed to *you*.**

Beta readers may not catch this; you will. If you are speeding through the climax in your own re-reads (flipping past pages instead of reading them) because the ending doesn't feel earned, more work is needed there specifically. The reader's experience of an ending is partly the writer's belief in it.

**You're avoiding a specific section.**

Most writers know which chapter they don't want to re-read. That chapter usually has a problem you haven't named yet. Read it. The problem will surface. Once named, it can be fixed.

:::check
Q: Last week you changed a comma in chapter five. This week you changed it back. What is the diagnosis?
- The prose still needs line editing, so keep going until the commas settle
-* The book is past the point where your edits change anything a reader will feel
- You should hand the chapter to a beta reader and revise it once more
X: Moving the same comma back and forth is the appearance of progress, not progress. It is the clearest observable sign that the manuscript has stopped changing in any way the reader will notice.
:::

## Beta readers: calibrating their feedback

Three beta readers is usually enough. Five is better. Ten is too many.

Each reader reads with their own preferences. Aggregating across many becomes noise: every comment is somebody's actual experience and not all of those experiences point in the same direction.

Trust the *patterns*, not the individual notes.

If one reader says the antagonist is unbelievable, consider it. If three say so, fix it. If three say so but in different ways (one finds the antagonist too cruel, another too sympathetic, the third unclear) the antagonist isn't *unbelievable*; the antagonist is *unspecific*. The pattern is the diagnosis. The individual notes are not.

The other discipline: do not solve every note. Solve the patterns. A reader who didn't like a chapter and a reader who loved the same chapter are giving you usable information about *which kind of reader your book is for*. That is not the same as a problem to fix.

:::check
Q: Three beta readers flag your antagonist: one finds him too cruel, one too sympathetic, one just confusing. What is the pattern telling you?
- The antagonist is unbelievable and needs to be softened
-* The antagonist is unspecific, so each reader is filling the gap differently
- The three notes cancel out, so no change is needed
X: When several readers react to the same element in contradictory ways, the element is underdefined. They are not disagreeing about a clear character; they are each inventing a different one to fill the blank.
:::

## Letting it go

At a certain point the manuscript stops being yours.

You did the work. Further work belongs to the editor, the publisher, the next book.

:::quote standalone
Books are not finished. They are released.
:::

The version you publish is the version you stopped working on; if you had kept going, the version a year later would be slightly different and probably not better. Probably the same set of strengths and weaknesses, with different specific words.

The mindset that lets you ship is *this is good enough for the reader I imagined writing for*. Not perfect. Not the best version possible. Good enough that the reader the book is for (the reader you have been writing toward since you started) will get what the book promises them.

If you have done the three ingredients (callback to [Module 1, Lesson 4](/learn/lesson/foundation-three-ingredients)) (character readers care about, conflict that resolves or earns its un-resolution, change the reader can feel) the book has done its job.

Ship it.

## The next-book remedy

The single most reliable cure for over-revising one book is starting the next one.

When the obsession has somewhere else to go, the previous book becomes shippable. The mind that was holding chapter twelve up to the light moves to drafting chapter one of something new. The previous book gets a final pass (usually a fast one) and goes out.

Beginning writers often resist this. They feel the next book should wait until the first is *done*.

It won't be done in the way they're thinking.

The next book is what makes the first one done. There is no other way through. A writer who insists on perfecting one book before starting another is a writer who has produced no books, ten years from now, while a slightly rougher version of the same writer has produced four.

Start the next book before you finish this one. Note ideas. Make outlines. Write the easy scene. By the time the current book is on the curve's tail end, the next book has enough momentum that putting the current one down is easy.

This is the loop the rest of the course was building toward. You used the framework (Module 1) to find a premise. You built the architecture (Module 2). You wrote the characters (Module 3). You rendered the world (Module 4). You drafted, revised, and shipped (Module 5).

Then you started over.

The course you are finishing is not the end of anything. It is one revolution of the loop you will spend the rest of your writing life inside.

## Try it

:::exercise
**Define "done" for your draft.** Write three specific, observable conditions that would tell you your current draft is shippable.

*Specific* means a beta reader could verify it. *Observable* means it doesn't depend on your mood that day.

Vague conditions don't count: *"when it's good"* is too vague, *"when I'm happy with it"* depends on the day, *"when the chapters all flow"* has no operational meaning.

Concrete conditions count: *"when three of three beta readers identify the same emotional climax"*, *"when I read chapter five aloud without stopping to fix anything"*, *"when I cannot, on a fresh read, name a structural change I would make."*

If you cannot write three concrete conditions, your sense of *done* is too vague to ever arrive at. This exercise is the start of having one.
:::

:::takeaway
- Revision has a diminishing returns curve: the first three to five passes do the real work, and the passes after that mostly move words around.
- Done looks like this: you shuffle commas, you rewrite passages you already approved, and beta readers run out of structural notes.
- Not done looks like this: you cannot say what the book is about, the ending feels rushed to you, or there is a chapter you avoid re-reading.
- Trust patterns across beta readers, never individual notes, and solve only the patterns.
- The most reliable cure for over-revising one book is starting the next one.
:::

## Further reading

- *An Autobiography* (Anthony Trollope, 1883). Trollope finished books at a rate that horrified his contemporaries and continues to horrify writers today. Read his account of starting a new novel the morning after finishing the previous one: the original demonstration of the *next-book remedy* in print. ([gutenberg.org/ebooks/5978](https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/5978))

lessons.title: Self-publishing vs traditional
lessons.content:
# Self-publishing vs traditional

Both paths exist for different reasons. Neither is *better*.

The right path is the one that fits this book, this writer, and this moment in their career. The writer's job is to make the choice deliberately, not by default, and to be willing to revisit it for the next book.

This lesson is the decision frame, not a verdict.

## The two paths exist for different reasons

Traditional and self-publishing are not better/worse versions of each other. They are different *systems* with different costs and different outcomes.

Most arguments about which is "better" are actually arguments about which system the speaker has succeeded inside. A traditionally published writer who's done well will tell you traditional is the path. A self-published writer who's done well will tell you self is the path. Both are telling you the truth about themselves; neither is telling you the truth about your book.

The honest version: each path serves different writers and different books. The skill is recognising which one this book wants.

:::video -f0biNdwifI | Lecture 12: Publishing Part One | Brandon Sanderson

## The tradeoffs, side by side

Here is the whole trade in one view.

:::example side-by-side | What each path gives you, and what it costs
[Traditional]
- Professional editorial work: developmental, copy, line.
- Distribution into physical bookstores.
- An advance, sometimes substantial, often modest.
- A publisher's name on the cover, plus marketing infrastructure.
- The cost: years of waiting, loss of control over cover and schedule, a modest royalty share, and the odds of getting through the gate at all.
[Self publishing]
- Speed: months instead of years.
- Control: cover, title, price, schedule, sequel timing.
- A much higher royalty rate per book.
- The ability to release, learn from readers, and iterate quickly.
- The cost: every other job in the system (editor, designer, formatter, marketer), weak bookstore access, and the discoverability fight against hundreds of new releases a day.
:::

The traditional system is not broken; it is selective and slow. Most queries don't land an agent; most agented manuscripts don't land a publisher. Writers who succeed in it usually have an exceptional manuscript, an exceptional pitch, or both, most often both.

The self-publishing system rewards persistence and consistent quality. Most successful self-published writers have multiple books out and a consistent output rate. They are running their own publishing house, on a small scale, with themselves as the only author. When they can't do a job themselves, they pay for it with money; when they can't pay, they pay with time, often badly the first time.

:::check
Q: A successful self-published author tells you self-publishing is obviously the better path. What does this lesson say their advice actually reveals?
- That self-publishing has better economics for most working writers
-* Which system that particular writer succeeded inside, not which path fits your book
- That traditional publishing has stopped taking on debut authors
X: Advocates of each path are usually describing their own survivorship. They are telling the truth about themselves, not about your book, which is why the decision has to be made per book, not by testimony.
:::

## The hybrid path

Many career writers do both, by book.

A literary novel through a traditional publisher. A genre series through self-publishing. A nonfiction project somewhere in between. The choice is per-book, not per-life.

The hybrid is not a compromise. It is a recognition that different books have different homes. A literary debut likely belongs traditional: the credibility, the reviews, the awards eligibility. A genre-series book five likely belongs self: you control the schedule, the royalties compound, the readers already know where to find you.

A writer who has built a reputation in one path can sometimes use it to negotiate better terms in the other. None of this is automatic; all of it requires the writer to know what each book actually needs.

:::video RGAnOKxBLhc | The Truth on Self Publishing vs Traditional Publishing | selfpublishing.com

## How to decide for *this* book

Three diagnostic questions.

**Is this book a single project or part of a series?**

Series writers do well in self-publishing. The royalty compounds; the reader who finishes book 1 can buy book 2 the same week; the writer's output rate becomes the marketing strategy. Standalone literary projects often want traditional infrastructure: the kind of attention a literary novel needs is harder to manufacture without a publisher.

**How long can I afford to wait?**

Traditional adds years between submission and shelf. Sometimes the writer's life or career genuinely cannot accommodate that timeline. Sometimes it can. The honest answer is the one that points toward path.

**What kind of validation do I need?**

Some writers need the gatekeeper's *yes* to keep going. Some don't. Both are legitimate; neither is heroic. The writer who needs to be told their work is good will struggle in self-publishing, where the only validation is sales and reviews. The writer who doesn't need permission can save themselves the years of querying.

There is no right answer in the abstract.

:::quote
The right answer is the one this book fits.
:::

## The common mistakes

**Assuming traditional = success.** A traditional book that sells five hundred copies and goes out of print after a year is not a success; a self-published book that builds a reader base over five years can outperform it commercially and culturally.

**Assuming self = failure.** The opposite mistake. Self-published writers with consistent output and small loyal audiences make a living more often than traditional writers do. The median published author across both paths is closer to *not* making a living than people imagine.

**Pretending the choice is moral.** It is not. It is a strategic decision about logistics, time, money, and fit. Writers who turn the choice into a moral position usually do so to flatter the path they've already taken. Treat it as logistics.

:::check
Q: You are planning book five of a genre series with readers who already know where to find you. Which way does the lesson's diagnostic point?
- Traditional, because later series books need bookstore distribution more than debuts do
-* Self-publishing, because series royalties compound and you control the release schedule your readers are waiting on
- Neither; the series should pause until an agent evaluates the whole arc
X: Series writers do well in self-publishing: the royalty compounds, readers can buy the next book the same week, and output rate becomes the marketing strategy. Standalone literary projects are the ones that more often want traditional infrastructure.
:::

## Historical note

Charles Dickens financed his own initial print run of *A Christmas Carol* in 1843. Chapman & Hall published it, but Dickens absorbed the production cost and took the royalty risk. Mark Twain owned his own subscription publishing house in the 1880s and used it to publish his work alongside others' (most famously the *Personal Memoirs of U.S. Grant*).

Self-publishing is older than the modern industry that defines itself against it. The mechanics now are different. The instinct (*I'll handle this myself, on my terms*) is the same.

## Try it

:::exercise
**Exercise 6.1.1: Three reasons each.** For your current book, write three reasons to go traditional and three reasons to go self-published. Be specific about your book and your situation; no generic answers.

Then read both lists. Identify which set is more *honest*: not which is more attractive, but which set names the actual conditions of *this* book at *this* moment.

The choice is then mostly made. Resist re-deciding it monthly; the indecision will cost you more than the wrong path would.
:::

:::takeaway
- Traditional and self-publishing are different systems, not ranked options; arguments about which is "better" are usually survivorship stories.
- Traditional buys editorial work, bookstore distribution, and credibility; it costs years, control, and royalty share.
- Self-publishing buys speed, control, and higher royalties; it costs you every other job in the system and the discoverability fight.
- Decide per book with three questions: series or standalone, how long you can wait, and what validation you need.
- The choice is logistics, not morality, and the answer can change on the next book.
:::

## Further reading

- *An Autobiography* (Anthony Trollope, 1883). The chapter on his contracts and royalties is the cleanest pre-1900 description of writer-publisher economics in English. ([gutenberg.org/ebooks/5978](https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/5978))
- *Life on the Mississippi* (Mark Twain, 1883). Twain's own writing career is the most famous Victorian example of the self-publishing instinct in action. ([gutenberg.org/ebooks/245](https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/245))
- **For current rates, contracts, and platform mechanics:** look up current royalty rates from your chosen retailer, current standard advance ranges from a recent debut-author roundup, and your local market's standard agent commission. The numbers shift; check the year you're publishing in.

lessons.title: Cover, blurb, and metadata
lessons.content:
# Cover, blurb, and metadata

A reader decides whether to look at your book in about a second and a half.

The cover, the blurb, and the metadata are how you make those 1.5 seconds work. The triad has to function together: a great cover with a vague blurb is half a sale lost; a sharp blurb on a wrong-genre cover is the same.

This lesson is mechanical. It is the part of publishing that responds best to deliberate work and worst to hope.

## The 1.5-second rule

On a retailer page or a bookstore shelf, your cover gets noticed (or not) in about a second and a half. If the reader's eye stops, they look at your title; if your title doesn't repel them, they read your blurb. If your blurb works, they buy.

The conversion rate at each step is brutal. Maybe one in twenty readers who scan past the cover pause on it. Maybe one in three of those click into the page. Maybe one in five of those read the blurb. Maybe one in three of those buy.

:::quote
The cover does not need to be artistically interesting. It needs to signal.
:::

## The cover signals genre, tone, and quality

**Genre.** A reader of literary fiction shouldn't see your literary novel and think it's a thriller. A reader of romance shouldn't see your romance and think it's horror. Genre is signalled by colour palette, typography, image style. These have settled into tight conventions inside each genre. The conventions are not a creative limitation; they are a contract with the reader. *This is the kind of book you came here for.*

**Tone.** Light or dark? Comic or serious? Modern or period? The cover answers in the first glance. A romance cover with a black background and a single thorned rose is making a tonal claim (*this is darker romance*) that will draw or repel different readers. The cover is the first promise the book makes.

**Quality.** Readers can tell. Hand-typeset titles, uneven kerning, stock photos with visible compositing seams. None of these are individually fatal. Together they signal *amateur*, and the reader closes the page.

The argument for hiring a designer is not aesthetic; it's economic. The cost of a competent genre-savvy cover is recoverable in five to fifty extra sales over the book's lifetime. The cost of a wrong cover is invisible: the readers who would have bought never saw the book.

*I designed it myself* is the most common reason a self-published book underperforms a comparable book that hired a cover.

The Victorians knew this. Cheap fiction in the mid-nineteenth century was bound in coloured boards (yellow, red, green) with each colour and each illustrative style coding genre to the buyer. The signalling system is not new.

## The blurb is a sales pitch, not a synopsis

A synopsis describes what happens in the book. A blurb describes why a stranger should read it.

The 3-paragraph blurb structure works for most genres:

**Hook.** One or two sentences that establish *who* the protagonist is and *what disturbs their normal*. Set up the want and the inciting incident.

**Stakes.** What's at risk. What's in the way. The specific obstacle. (Callback to [Module 3, Lesson 1](/learn/lesson/characters-protagonist-wound) and [Module 1, Lesson 4](/learn/lesson/foundation-three-ingredients): the wound, the want, the conflict, all making it onto the back cover.)

**Promise.** The kind of book this is. Not what happens; *what the reader will feel*. *A propulsive thriller about… A tender debut about… A gothic mystery about…*

A 50-word blurb is harder than a 200-word blurb because the writer has to choose. The exercise at the end of this lesson is the choosing.

## A working blurb

Here is the structure in action. Tap through the three moves; each is one paragraph of the same 83-word blurb.

:::example click-toggle | One blurb, three moves
[Hook]
When a librarian inherits her grandmother's locked attic, she expects three crates of mildewed letters. She finds the answer to a disappearance that her family has been pretending didn't happen for fifty years.
[Stakes]
Reopening the case will cost her the only relative who still speaks to her, and it will mean asking the dead woman a question her family has spent half a century not asking.
[Promise]
A literary mystery about the lies families keep, and what survives the keeping.
:::

The hook gives a protagonist (a librarian) and an inciting incident (the inheritance) in two sentences. The reader knows who and what within ten seconds.

The stakes paragraph names what's at risk (*the only relative who still speaks to her*) and what the obstacle is (*asking the dead woman a question her family has spent half a century not asking*). Not generic stakes. Not *can she find the truth?* Specific stakes the reader can hold in mind.

The promise paragraph tells the reader what kind of book this is. *A literary mystery about the lies families keep.* Genre + theme + texture. A reader who wants a literary mystery about family secrets buys; a reader who wants a fast-paced thriller doesn't, and that is exactly what the blurb is for. The blurb's job is partly to *exclude* readers who would not be happy with this book.

Most beginner blurbs leak at the third paragraph. They re-summarise the plot instead of naming the kind of book it is.

Test: read your third paragraph aloud and ask, *would this make a stranger want to start chapter one?* If it makes them want to know what happens next, the third paragraph is wrong; that is the second paragraph's job. The third paragraph is selling the *experience*.

:::check
Q: Your blurb's final paragraph leaves readers desperate to find out what happens next. According to the lesson, what is wrong?
- Nothing; making readers want to know what happens is the whole point of a blurb
-* That pull belongs to the stakes paragraph; the final paragraph should name the kind of experience the book is
- The final paragraph should reveal the ending so readers can make an informed purchase
X: The third move is the promise: genre, theme, texture, what the reader will feel. Curiosity about plot is the second paragraph's job. When the third paragraph re-summarises plot, the blurb leaks exactly where beginners' blurbs usually leak.
:::

## Metadata: discoverability infrastructure

**Categories** are how retailers organise their catalogue. *Literary fiction → Family life* is a different shelf from *Mystery → Cozy*. A book miscategorised lives on the wrong shelf and dies there. Pick categories where readers of *your* kind of book actually browse, not the categories that sound prestigious, but the ones where your book is comparable to the others on the shelf.

**Keywords** are how readers search. Generic words (*love*, *adventure*) are crowded; specific words (*Victorian London*, *unreliable narrator*, *Yorkshire moors*) place you next to the books you'd be compared with. Keyword research is mechanical, not creative; do it once per book, properly, and don't think about it again.

**Title and subtitle** carry searchable terms. *Memoir* in the subtitle of a memoir, *novel* if helpful for genre clarity. The title carries voice; the subtitle carries findability.

**Series naming** matters if you're writing in a series. Make the series name searchable. Make the book number visible. *Book 1 of the Hollow Hills Mysteries* in the metadata; the cover follows.

## The triad must work together

A perfect cover with a vague blurb loses sales. A sharp blurb on a wrong-genre cover loses sales. Mismatched metadata puts both on the wrong shelf, where neither helps.

Test: have a stranger look at your cover for 1.5 seconds, then read your blurb, then check what genre they think you wrote. If their answer matches your intent, the triad is working. If it doesn't (if the cover said literary fiction and the blurb said thriller, or vice versa) the triad has a leak.

The triad's failure mode is silent. Books with leaking triads don't get bad reviews; they get *no* reviews, because they reach no readers. The diagnostic is invisible without doing the test.

:::check
Q: A book whose cover, blurb, and metadata contradict each other will most likely show which symptom?
- A wave of angry reviews from readers who feel misled by the cover
-* Near silence: few sales and almost no reviews, because the book never reaches its readers
- Strong first-week sales that collapse once word of mouth catches up
X: The triad's failure mode is silent. A leaking triad doesn't anger readers; it never reaches them, so the book gets no reviews at all. That is why the stranger test has to be run deliberately before launch.
:::

## Try it

:::exercise
**Exercise 6.2.1: Three blurbs in three lengths.** Write three versions of your book's blurb:

- **200 words.** The full version. Hook, stakes, promise, with room for nuance.
- **100 words.** The trimmed version. Each phrase has to do more work.
- **50 words.** The hardest version. You will have to cut everything that isn't load-bearing.

The 50-word version is the most useful. It is what you'd send in an email to a friend asking what your book is about. If you can't make 50 words work, your hook isn't sharp enough. The diagnosis is upstream of the blurb.
:::

:::takeaway
- A cover gets about 1.5 seconds; its job is to signal genre, tone, and quality, not to be art.
- A blurb is a sales pitch, not a synopsis: hook, stakes, promise, in three paragraphs.
- The promise paragraph sells the experience and deliberately excludes readers who would not be happy with the book.
- Metadata (categories, keywords, title, series naming) is infrastructure; do the research once per book, properly.
- Cover, blurb, and metadata must tell the same genre story; run the stranger test, because the triad fails silently.
:::

## Further reading

- For the historical genre-signalling argument, read about Victorian "yellow-back" novels and their colour-coded covers. Any general history of nineteenth-century publishing covers the topic. (Not a single PD title; it's a phenomenon.)
- **For current cover trends in your genre:** browse the top fifty bestsellers in your genre on the major retailer once a month for three months. The conventions are visible at that scale and shift over years. Note the colour palettes, the typography, the image styles. Brief your designer with this study, not with your favourite book from ten years ago.

lessons.title: Building an audience
lessons.content:
# Building an audience

*Audience* comes before *marketing*.

Marketing is what you do to people who already know who you are. The skill of building readers (readers who will pay for your *next* book before this one launches) is the skill nobody teaches and almost everyone needs.

This lesson is the principle, not the platform.

## Audience is not marketing

Marketing is a transactional moment: I have a book; you might buy it.

Audience is a relationship: I have written things; you have liked them; you would pay attention to the next one.

:::quote
Marketing without audience is cold-calling. Marketing with audience is sending a message to people who already raised their hand.
:::

The conversion rates are not in the same universe.

Most first-time authors confuse the two. They market hard at launch and audience-build never. By book 3 the gap has compounded: the writer who built audience while drafting has six hundred engaged readers; the writer who marketed at launch has the same starting position they had three books ago.

## The newsletter is the only platform you own

Social media is rented attention. The platform decides who sees your post. The algorithm changes; your reach drops; you cannot move your followers to the next platform when this one breaks.

A newsletter is owned. The list belongs to you. The platform (whichever subscription newsletter platform you use) is interchangeable; the list is portable. If the platform goes under or gets worse, you export the list and move it.

For writers, the newsletter is not optional. It is the single most reliable way to reach readers who will care about book 2.

Start it before book 1 if possible. During book 1 if not. Not after. *After* is the most common mistake; the launch happens to people who don't know you yet, because there was no list to tell.

What goes in the newsletter is less important than that it exists. Behind-the-scenes notes. The next book's cover. A short story. The newsletter is a habit, not a content strategy. Twice a month is plenty. Once a month is fine. Twice a year is too rare; the list cools.

:::check
Q: Why does the lesson call a newsletter "owned" and social media "rented"?
- A newsletter costs money to run while social media platforms are free
-* The list is portable and belongs to you; a social platform sits between you and the reader and decides who sees your posts
- Newsletter subscribers are legally your customers, while followers are the platform's customers
X: Ownership here means control of the relationship. You can export a list and move platforms; you cannot take your followers with you, and the platform can throttle your reach at any time.
:::

## Why social media is rented attention

Three problems with social media as audience-building, all real.

**You don't own the relationship.** The platform sits between you and the reader. The platform can change the rules at any time and frequently does.

**Reach is throttled.** A post that would have reached 30% of your followers a few years ago might now reach 2%. The number was higher when you started. It will be lower next year.

**Engagement metrics are not readers.** Ten thousand followers who scroll past your posts are worth less than two hundred newsletter subscribers who open every email.

Social media is useful for *finding* readers: people who don't know you exist, scroll past, and one day click. It is not useful for *keeping* them. Move them to the newsletter. That's the only thing the social presence is for.

## The honest math

One hundred engaged readers > ten thousand indifferent followers.

This is not encouragement. It is arithmetic.

One hundred readers who buy your next book at full price the week it launches generates more revenue than ten thousand followers who never bought your last one. A small list of buyers builds momentum. A large vanity-metric audience does not.

The implication: stop chasing follower counts. Chase opt-ins. The reader who gives you their email is telling you something the follower never did: *I want to hear from you specifically.* That signal is what an audience is.

## Two hundred is enough to start

A small number of readers who would pay for your next book is worth more than a large number who barely know you exist. **Two hundred is enough to start.**

With two hundred engaged readers, your launch is no longer a stranger event. The book lands in front of a small group that already cares. They review. Some tell three friends each. The launch starts with momentum the empty-list version doesn't have.

Two hundred is also achievable in a year of steady audience-building. Less than one new subscriber per day. Fifty per quarter. The math is not heroic.

The heroism is in not stopping when the list grows by twelve in a slow month. The heroism is in writing the same kind of newsletter to a list of fourteen that you'd write to a list of fourteen thousand. The list grows the same way books are written: daily, slowly, until one day it isn't slow anymore.

## When to start

Before book 1 if possible. The optimal moment is two years before launch, when the writer has time to build slowly and the list compounds.

During book 1 is the realistic window. Most writers start audience-building in month four of drafting, not before. That works. The list is smaller at launch but it is not zero.

After book 1 launches is the worst time. The launch happens to strangers; momentum is impossible; the writer learns the lesson three years late and starts the next launch with a list of zero again. The cure is the next-book remedy from [M5 L5](/learn/lesson/process-when-to-stop): start the next book and the next list at the same time.

:::check
Q: When is the best realistic time to start building your reader list?
- After book 1 launches, once you finally have something concrete to point people to
-* While drafting book 1, so the launch lands in front of people who already know you
- Only after your social following is large enough to convert into subscribers
X: Starting after launch is the most common mistake: the launch happens to strangers and momentum is impossible. Before book 1 is optimal, during book 1 is realistic, and the list does not need social media to start growing.
:::

## Try it

:::exercise
**Exercise 6.3.1: The reader-facing identity.** Write a 200-word *about the author* page. Not your bio. Your reader-facing identity.

The bio version says *I have an MFA. I live in Maine. I have two cats.* These are facts. They do not tell a stranger why they should read your next book.

The reader-facing version answers a single question: *why would a reader who liked your first book want to read your second one?*

Possible angles:

- The kind of stories you write (genre + tone + texture).
- The themes you keep returning to.
- The reader you imagine writing for.
- One specific, honest thing about how you approach the work.

Two hundred words. The exercise is not the page itself; the exercise is figuring out what you'd actually say. The page comes after.
:::

:::takeaway
- Marketing is a transaction with strangers; audience is a relationship with readers who already raised their hand.
- The newsletter is the only platform you own: the list is portable, the reach is unthrottled, the habit matters more than the content.
- Social media is for finding readers, not keeping them; move them to the list.
- One hundred engaged readers beat ten thousand indifferent followers, and two hundred is enough to start.
- Start the list before or during book 1, never after; the launch should not happen to strangers.
:::

## Further reading

- **For current platform mechanics:** review the help documentation for your subscription newsletter platform of choice. Most are free up to the early-list size where you'll start. The mechanics shift annually; check current.
- *An Autobiography* (Anthony Trollope, 1883). Trollope wrote about his readership constantly; the *Autobiography* contains the cleanest pre-1900 description of an author thinking about the people who buy his books. Read with the substitution of *newsletter* for *circulating library subscription* and the principle survives. ([gutenberg.org/ebooks/5978](https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/5978))

lessons.title: Marketing your first book
lessons.content:
# Marketing your first book

Marketing for first-time authors is mostly waste.

Accept this. The book itself is the best marketing for the *next* book. The first launch is an exercise in three honest tactics, three honest refusals, and a long view: book 3 starts when book 1 ships.

This lesson contains the won't-do list. **The won't-do list matters more than the do list.** That is not a literary flourish; it is the lesson.

## Most first-launch marketing is waste

First-time authors spend money on launch marketing and most of it produces nothing. Cold ads to readers who don't know your name. Blog tours where the blogs have audiences smaller than your newsletter. Paid placement that disappears in the first 48 hours.

This is not pessimism. It is the math. You have not yet built the conditions in which marketing dollars convert. The conditions are: an audience that knows you ([Lesson 3](/learn/lesson/publishing-audience)), a cover that signals genre ([Lesson 2](/learn/lesson/publishing-cover-blurb)), a book that delivers (Modules 1 to 5). When those three are in place, marketing dollars work. Before they are, marketing is friction without compounding.

The fix is not *more marketing*. The fix is *honest marketing*: the small list of things that actually work for a first book, plus a frame that values consistency over one big launch.

:::video IX6LggnkxG8 | How to Publish a Book, 45 Years of Experience | Jerry B. Jenkins

## Three honest tactics

**The newsletter announcement.**

Your list (Lesson 3) is the highest-converting audience you'll ever have. A pre-launch sequence (three to five emails over the weeks before publication) converts at rates that paid advertising cannot match. Without a list, this tactic doesn't exist; with one, it is the foundation of every honest launch.

The sequence:

:::checklist
- Announce the cover
- Share an excerpt
- Give the launch date
- Ask for pre-orders
- Send the launch-day email
:::

Each email lands with people who already opted in. The conversion is not extraordinary; the audience pre-sorted itself.

**Asking for reviews, directly, from readers you respect.**

Twenty thoughtful reviews on launch week change the book's discoverability on most retailers. Most of those reviews come from a writer asking specific readers (people who already enjoyed your work, beta readers, newsletter subscribers) for the favour.

Honest, transparent ask. Not a brigade. Not a swap. Just: *I valued your read; would you write a few sentences if you mean them?* Most readers will. The few who say no are not insulted by the ask.

**Genuine presence in your genre's communities.**

Genre readers cluster. There are forums, podcasts, review sites, blogs, occasionally physical conventions. Show up to the ones you care about. Read other writers' books in your genre. Comment thoughtfully on conversations. Be a person.

Years before launch is best; months are workable; the day before launch is too late. *Genuine* is the operative word. The community can tell when someone shows up two weeks before launch with a book to push, and it does not respond well.

## Three tactics that don't work

**Cold social media posts.**

*Hello strangers, please buy my book* posts perform at single-digit click rates and zero-digit conversion rates. The audience for this kind of post is the writer's existing followers, who would have heard about the book anyway. The strangers scroll past.

Posting *about* the book to your existing social audience is fine. You have a relationship with them. Posting *at* strangers is what doesn't work.

**Paid ads without an audience.**

Paid ads work when you have *retargeting data* and *conversion infrastructure*, neither of which a first-time author has. Most first-launch ads are unprofitable; the writer cannot tell because they don't have the data to measure conversion against, and they spend three to ten times the eventual royalty earned to acquire each reader.

Ads are useful at book 3, after you've built the data and the cover designs and the audience that ads can amplify. They are usually unprofitable at book 1.

**The blog tour.**

Twelve guest posts on twelve writing blogs over launch week. Each blog has a tiny audience. The total reach is below the writer's own newsletter. The time spent could have been a week of work on book 2.

Blog tours used to work, in the era when blog audiences were larger and writing blogs were the centre of the conversation. They mostly don't now. The exception: if the blog has a substantial audience in *your specific genre*, the post might be worth the effort. Most blogs do not.

:::check
Q: Why do paid ads usually fail at book 1?
- Ad platforms give unknown authors worse placement than established names
-* The writer has no retargeting data or conversion infrastructure, so the spend can't be measured or amplified and usually exceeds the royalties it earns
- Readers distrust advertised books and prefer organic recommendations
X: Ads work when there is data to target with and infrastructure to convert through. A first-time author has neither, which is why ads become useful around book 3, after the audience and the data exist for ads to amplify.
:::

## The thread connecting the three failures

Each of the three tactics that don't work treats *strangers* as the launch audience.

Strangers convert poorly. The honest tactics treat *readers who already know you* as the launch audience. Two of them (newsletter, genre-community presence) are about deepening relationships you already have; one (asking beta readers for reviews) is about cashing in on relationships you've already built.

:::quote
The marketing that works at book 1 is the marketing of relationships, not the marketing of reach.
:::

## The won't-do list matters more than the do list

This is the lesson, stated explicitly: **the won't-do list is the more important of the two.**

For every tactic offered (paid course, paid promo site, expensive ad service, paid review service), the writer who has thought about the won't-do list has the answer ready. *Not for this book. Not at this scale of audience. Not at this stage of career.*

Without a won't-do list, every shiny tactic is a temptation. The launch becomes a vortex: three months on tactics that produce nothing, while book 2 sits unwritten.
